one_line_takeaway: Adding a perceptual-loss-optimized 'interrogation' pre-processing step before an unmodified gradient-based membership detector (GLiR) raises AUC from near-chance (0.53-0.62) to 0.65-0.89 and boosts TPR@1%FPR by roughly an order of magnitude on ViT-4 and AlexNet, but the benefit is architecture- and detector-specific, giving little to no gain on DenseNet/ResNet-18 or on non-gradient detectors (SIF, LAEQ, IA).
key_finding: LeakBoost+GLiR produces large, architecture-dependent gains. On CIFAR-10, AUC rises from baseline near-chance levels (~0.53-0.58 across SIF/LAEQ/GLiR/IA) to 0.811 on ViT-4 and 0.885 on AlexNet, with TPR@1%FPR rising from <=1.3% (baselines) to 11.80% (ViT-4) and 20.15% (AlexNet), and TPR@0.1%FPR rising from <0.2% to 2.39% and 3.05% respectively. On CIFAR-100, LeakBoost reaches AUC 0.838 (ViT-4, vs. baselines ~0.50-0.62) and 0.653 (AlexNet, vs. baselines ~0.57-0.67), with TPR@1%FPR of 10.60% and 30.49%. By contrast, gains are minor or negative on deeper/more regularized CNNs: on DenseNet, LeakBoost's AUC (0.546 C-10 / 0.591 C-100) is at or below the best baseline (0.558 C-10 / ~0.64 C-100); on ResNet-18, AUC drops slightly on CIFAR-10 (0.530 vs. ~0.58 for baselines) though TPR@0.1%FPR ticks up marginally on CIFAR-100 (0.28% vs. <=0.21%). A component analysis (boosting SIF/LAEQ/IA instead of GLiR) shows the interrogation step's benefit is highly detector-specific: boosting improves GLiR's pAUC@1% by ~1e-3 (largest on ViT-4/AlexNet) but yields only marginal, often negative changes (within ±1e-5 to 1e-4) for SIF/LAEQ/IA, attributed to GLiR's explicit dependence on gradient statistics that the interrogation optimization directly reshapes. Sensitivity analysis on ViT-4 and AlexNet shows: (i) for ViT-4, short optimization (80 steps) and a low learning rate (0.05) yield the strongest signal, with the signal nearly vanishing by 200 steps, and deeper (mid/late) layers leak more than early layers, while combining all layers underperforms the best single subset; (ii) AlexNet's sensitivity is far less stable/monotonic and dataset-dependent, with strong leakage confined to specific hyperparameter regimes (e.g., 120 steps, LR 0.05, clipping enabled on CIFAR-100), and late layers generally the most informative. A brief defense experiment applies activation-space obfuscation (layer normalization + additive Gaussian noise on intermediate representations) and finds negligible degradation of LeakBoost+GLiR's performance at the tested noise levels, i.e., this obfuscation defense does not mitigate the attack.
core_contribution: The paper introduces LeakBoost, a white-box membership inference attack (MIA) framework that adds an active 'interrogation' pre-processing step in front of an existing, unmodified membership detector. Given a candidate image x and full white-box access to a target classifier, LeakBoost initializes a synthetic image from Gaussian noise and optimizes it (via Adam, using a perceptual/activation-space MSE loss computed over a chosen set of internal layers) so that its internal activations match those of x under the target model. The resulting 'interrogation image' is then fed, unchanged, into an off-the-shelf membership detector (the detector itself is not modified). The core claim is that this boosted input amplifies latent member/non-member representational differences that the detector alone cannot see in the original input, particularly for detectors that read gradient information (GLiR).
threat_model: White-box MIA: the attacker has full access to a trained target model T_theta, including its parameters theta, and can compute arbitrary forward/backward passes, extract intermediate activations e_l(x) at any layer, and compute gradients w.r.t. inputs or internal representations. The attacker submits both natural and synthetically generated (interrogation) queries to the model, and has auxiliary data from the same input distribution to approximate non-member behavior, but does not have direct access to the actual training set or individual training examples. The paper frames this as corresponding to insider access, debugging/evaluation interfaces, or third-party auditing of models trained on sensitive/proprietary data.
limitations: The paper's own text and results surface several limitations: (1) The attack's effectiveness is highly architecture-dependent and, for deeper/more heavily regularized CNNs (DenseNet, ResNet-18), gains are minor, inconsistent, or occasionally negative in overall AUC -- the authors themselves state 'For deeper CNNs such as DenseNet and ResNet-18, benefits are minor or inconsistent, limited mostly to slight gains in the extreme low-FPR regime.' (2) The boosting benefit is not detector-agnostic in practice despite being architecturally 'detector-agnostic': only GLiR (a gradient-based detector) benefits substantially; SIF, LAEQ, and IA show negligible or negative changes. (3) Hyperparameter sensitivity is severe and non-monotonic for at least one architecture (AlexNet), meaning the attack requires per-model, per-dataset tuning (chosen via a separate validation split) rather than a single robust configuration. (4) Evaluation is restricted to CIFAR-10/CIFAR-100 at 32x32 resolution with 4 specific architectures; no results are shown for larger/real-world image domains (e.g., faces, medical scans) that the introduction uses to motivate real-world stakes. (5) The reliable FPR floor is 0.1% (10 false positives out of the 10K/10K test split), so claims below that regime are explicitly called unstable by the authors themselves. (6) Only one defense (activation-space noise obfuscation) is tested, and only against LeakBoost+GLiR; other defenses (differential privacy, adversarial training) are left to future work, not evaluated empirically. (7) The paper does not report the computational overhead of the interrogation optimization (T forward/backward passes per candidate) relative to baseline detector cost, despite characterizing the method as 'computationally lightweight.'

quantitative_results: CIFAR-100 AlexNet: AUC 0.653 (LeakBoost) vs. 0.577-0.675 (baselines); TPR@1%FPR 30.49% (LeakBoost) vs. 0.98-1.56% (baselines)
CIFAR-10 DenseNet: AUC 0.546 (LeakBoost) vs. 0.540-0.558 (baselines) -- LeakBoost roughly matches/underperforms
CIFAR-10 ResNet-18: AUC 0.530 (LeakBoost) vs. 0.548-0.586 (baselines) -- LeakBoost underperforms on overall AUC
Boosting Delta pAUC@1%FPR: ~1e-3 for GLiR (largest on ViT-4/AlexNet) vs. within +/-1e-5 to 1e-4 for SIF/LAEQ/IA when boosted
Best ViT-4 hyperparameters (validation-selected): Late layers, 80 steps, LR 0.05, clipping True (CIFAR-10); Mid layers, 80 steps, LR 0.05, clipping True (CIFAR-100)
Best AlexNet hyperparameters (validation-selected): Mid layers, 120 steps, LR 0.05, clipping True (CIFAR-10); Late layers, 120 steps, LR 0.05, clipping True (CIFAR-100)
Target model clean-task accuracy (test set): ResNet-18 89.47% (C-10)/59.87% (C-100); AlexNet 80.05%/39.72%; DenseNet 89.60%/60.88%; ViT-4 67.57%/37.75%
Reliable FPR floor for the 10K/10K test split: 1% (100 false positives) and 0.1% (10 false positives); below 0.1% deemed unstable by the authors
baselines_compared: GLiR (Gradient Likelihood Ratio, Leemann et al. 2023) - white-box gradient-based likelihood-ratio test
SIF (Self-Influence Functions, Cohen & Giryes 2024) - influence-function-based white-box MIA
LAEQ (Del Grosso et al. 2022) - adversarial-perturbation-magnitude (L2 norm variant used here) white-box MIA
IA (Input Activations, Cretu et al. 2024) - shadow-model meta-classifier using penultimate-layer activations, loss gradients, and input-activation features (5 shadow models trained per target)
